FT_IC1.evidence: Uses ABCD Study Year 3 follow-up (2019-2021), ages 11-14 years old, N=9989

FT_IC2.evidence: Outcome is cyberbullying victimization but no separate smartphone/social media exposure variable. Paper asks 'Have you ever been cyberbullied...on social media' but this is the outcome, not a measured SM variable separate from the outcome.

FT_IC3.evidence: Logistic regression with transgender identity (OR 2.24, 95% CI 1.22-4.10, p=0.009) and gender-questioning (OR 1.91, 95% CI 1.05-3.47, p=0.035) as predictors of lifetime cyberbullying victimization

FT_IC4.evidence: Odds ratios and 95% confidence intervals reported in Table 2

FT_IC5.evidence: Cross-sectional empirical analysis using logistic regression on ABCD data

FT_IC6.evidence: Published journal article in Academic Pediatrics, 2025; appears to be original research

FT_EC7_reason: Paper fails FT_IC2 (no separate smartphone/SM exposure variable measured); EC7 only applies if FT_IC1-IC6 all pass
notes: Paper measures cyberbullying victimization (outcome occurring on digital platforms) but does not measure smartphone or social media use as a separate exposure variable. This is cyberbullying-only with no separately-measured phone/SM variable, failing FT_IC2 per E6.

FT_IC1.evidence: Uses ABCD Study Year 3 (2019-2021), ages 11-15 years old, N=10,092

FT_IC2.evidence: Extensively measures social media use: platform-specific accounts (TikTok 67.1%, Instagram 66.0%, YouTube 64.7%, Snapchat 60.3%), secret social media accounts (6.3%), Social Media Addiction Questionnaire (SMAQ), average daily social media time (0.19 h/d)

FT_IC3.evidence: Purely descriptive study. Chi-square and t-tests compare demographic characteristics across SM use patterns, but no inferential tests linking SM variables to non-SM health/behavioral outcomes. No hypothesis testing of SM → outcome associations.

FT_IC4.evidence: Percentages and means reported throughout (e.g., 69.5% had at least one SM account, 0.19 h/d average SM time)

FT_IC5.evidence: Descriptive/prevalence study only. No empirical analysis linking SM use to external health or behavioral outcomes. This is a measurement/characterization paper without outcome analysis.

FT_IC6.evidence: Published journal article in Academic Pediatrics, 2025; appears to be original research

FT_EC7_reason: Paper fails FT_IC3 (no inferential testing of SM associations with outcomes) and FT_IC5 (descriptive prevalence study without outcome analysis)
notes: E8: Descriptive vs inferential — only means/percentages reported, no hypothesis tests with p-values comparing SM use to outcomes. E10: This is a descriptive resource paper characterizing SM use patterns with no external outcome analysis.

FT_IC1.evidence: Uses ABCD Study prospective data, Year 2 (ages 11-12, 2018-2020) → Year 3, N=8006

FT_IC2.evidence: Measures eight screen modalities including social media, texting, and video chatting. Social media (mean 0.7 h/d), texting (mean 0.7 h/d), video chatting (mean 0.5 h/d) are specific phone/SM variables.

FT_IC3.evidence: Logistic regression analyses: social media (AOR 1.20, 95% CI 1.14-1.26, p<0.001), texting (AOR 1.18, 95% CI 1.12-1.24, p<0.001), video chatting (AOR 1.09, 95% CI 1.03-1.16, p<0.001) prospectively associated with substance use experimentation. Multiple inferential tests reported.

FT_IC4.evidence: Adjusted odds ratios and 95% confidence intervals reported in Figure 1 and text for all screen modalities and substance outcomes

FT_IC5.evidence: Prospective longitudinal empirical analysis using logistic regression with Year 2 screen time predicting Year 3 substance use experimentation

FT_IC6.evidence: Published journal article in Drug and Alcohol Dependence, 2025; appears to be original research

FT_EC7_reason: Social media/texting/video chatting are independent variables (IVs), not dependent variables. Outcome is substance use experimentation (non-SM behavioral outcome). EC7 only applies when SM is DV with demographic-only predictors.
notes: E1: SM/phone variables have their own coefficients reported (social media, texting, video chatting all have separate AORs and CIs), satisfying FT_IC3. Prospective design with behavioral outcome (substance experimentation) clearly separates this from EC7 category.
